SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper supports its main empirical claims with experiments (10-fold cross-validation, integration into Dcoref and CoNLL scorer results) and reports concrete numeric improvements (e.g. 13 absolute F1 gain for MC identification and +4 CoNLL F1 when integrated into Dcoref). The learning setup and classifiers are described (LIBSVM, linear kernel, class weight) and the authors use standard metrics. Missing are statistical significance tests and some dataset details are withheld (WCR described only at high level), which are minor gaps but do not appear to invalidate the main conclusions.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The focus on identifying the 'main concept' mentions in Wikipedia and exploiting Wikipedia markup/Freebase for this specific subtask is a targeted contribution that is not widely studied according to the authors. However, the methods (feature engineering + SVMs, using Wikipedia/Freebase signals) largely reuse existing ideas from prior work cited (e.g., wikification, entity attributes) and amount to an incremental, domain-specific application rather than a fundamentally new algorithmic idea.

CLARITY.justification: The paper is organized in a standard way (approach, features, dataset, experiments, conclusion) and many experimental details are clearly stated (feature families, classifier choice, metrics). Nevertheless, some important elements are deferred or referenced to the non-anonymized version (WCR dataset details), figures are referenced but not present in the excerpt, and some feature descriptions are high-level ("a few hundred features") which makes parts harder to follow in detail.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The experiments compare multiple strong baselines and show both standalone and pipeline gains, and the authors discuss dataset construction and sampling variety. However, the dataset is small (30 documents), ablation details and per-feature-family quantitative contributions are only hinted at rather than fully reported in this excerpt, and limitations (small resource, withheld details) are acknowledged but not deeply analyzed.

IMPACT.justification: Adapting coreference resolution to Wikipedia and reliably identifying main-concept mentions can be useful for many downstream tasks and the improvements reported (e.g. +4 CoNLL F1 in a pipeline) are practically relevant. Still, the work targets a fairly specific subproblem/genre and appears unlikely to be transformative for coreference research at large without broader generalization beyond Wikipedia.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: The topic (coreference resolution tailored to Wikipedia) squarely fits top-tier NLP venues and the paper uses community-accepted methods and evaluations (CoNLL scorer, comparisons to Dcoref and state-of-the-art systems). The problem, methods, and metrics align with expectations for NLP/ML conferences.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper runs and reports results from several recent state-of-the-art coreference systems (Dcoref, Durrett & Klein variants, Cort, Scoref) on the WCR dataset and evaluates with standard metrics; it also shows the effect of integrating the proposed classifier into Dcoref. Some comparisons are omitted in later sections (authors state they only include two systems in the sequel) and statistical testing or deeper error analyses comparing methods are not shown in the excerpt.

REPLICABILITY.justification: The paper provides many implementation details (LIBSVM, linear kernel, class weighting, 10-fold CV, feature family descriptions) and states an intention to release datasets and mention dumps. However, the full WCR dataset details are deferred to a non-anonymized version and many feature-level specifics are summarized ("a few hundred features"), so reproducing exact results would require additional released artifacts or more complete descriptions.
